---
title: "COMFORTER study analysis"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
date: last-modified
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

from pathlib import Path

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import Markdown, display
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import (
    average_precision_score,
    brier_score_loss,
    precision_recall_curve,
    roc_auc_score,
    roc_curve,
)

ROOT = Path("/Users/ac/comforter")
PUB_ROOT = ROOT / "artifacts" / "internal_external_validation_death_publication"
REMAIN_DIR = PUB_ROOT / "publication_ready_remain_logistic"
MODELS_DIR = PUB_ROOT / "publication_ready"
MERGED_MODELS_DIR = ROOT / "artifacts" / "internal_external_validation_death_merged_selected_combined"
NEWS_LOGISTIC_DIR = ROOT / "artifacts" / "internal_external_validation_death_news_logistic"

SITE_LABEL = {
    "Site_1": "Site 1",
    "Site_2": "Site 2",
    "Site_3": "Site 3",
    "Site_4": "Site 4",
}
SITE_ORDER = {"Site_1": 1, "Site_2": 2, "Site_3": 3, "Site_4": 4}
SITE_KEYS = ["Site_1", "Site_2", "Site_3", "Site_4"]
SITE_COLORS = {
    "Site_1": "#4E79A7",
    "Site_2": "#F28E2B",
    "Site_3": "#59A14F",
    "Site_4": "#E15759",
}
MODEL_ORDER = {
    "NEWS logistic": 1,
    "TabICL": 2,
    "Group lasso": 3,
    "Group ridge": 4,
    "AutoGluon": 5,
    "Random forest": 6,
    "TabICL (Expanded feature set)": 7,
    "AutoGluon (Expanded feature set)": 8,
    "Random forest (Expanded feature set)": 9,
}


def fmt(x: float, n: int = 3) -> str:
    return f"{float(x):.{n}f}"


def fmt_ci(est: float, lo: float, hi: float, n: int = 3) -> str:
    return f"{fmt(est, n)} ({fmt(lo, n)} to {fmt(hi, n)})"


def fmt_range(lo: float, hi: float, n: int = 3) -> str:
    return f"{fmt(lo, n)} to {fmt(hi, n)}"


def _plot_roc_by_site(predictions: pd.DataFrame, title: str) -> None:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 6.2))
    ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], linestyle="--", color="#9CA3AF", linewidth=1.3, label="No-discrimination")
    for site in SITE_KEYS:
        site_df = predictions.loc[predictions["test_site"] == site]
        if site_df.empty:
            continue
        y_true = site_df["y_true"].to_numpy(dtype=int)
        y_prob = site_df["proba_death"].to_numpy(dtype=float)
        fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_true, y_prob)
        auc = roc_auc_score(y_true, y_prob)
        ax.plot(
            fpr,
            tpr,
            color=SITE_COLORS[site],
            linewidth=2.4,
            label=f"{SITE_LABEL.get(site, site)} (AUC {auc:.3f})",
        )
    ax.set(xlim=(0.0, 1.0), ylim=(0.0, 1.0), xlabel="False positive rate", ylabel="True positive rate", title=title)
    ax.legend(loc="lower right", frameon=True, framealpha=0.92, fontsize=9.0)
    fig.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    plt.close(fig)


def _plot_pr_by_site(predictions: pd.DataFrame, title: str) -> None:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 6.2))
    prevalence = float(predictions["y_true"].mean())
    ax.plot(
        [0, 1],
        [prevalence, prevalence],
        linestyle="--",
        color="#9CA3AF",
        linewidth=1.3,
        label=f"Prevalence = {prevalence:.3f}",
    )
    for site in SITE_KEYS:
        site_df = predictions.loc[predictions["test_site"] == site]
        if site_df.empty:
            continue
        y_true = site_df["y_true"].to_numpy(dtype=int)
        y_prob = site_df["proba_death"].to_numpy(dtype=float)
        precision, recall, _ = precision_recall_curve(y_true, y_prob)
        ap = average_precision_score(y_true, y_prob)
        ax.plot(
            recall,
            precision,
            color=SITE_COLORS[site],
            linewidth=2.4,
            label=f"{SITE_LABEL.get(site, site)} (AP {ap:.3f})",
        )
    ax.set(xlim=(0.0, 1.0), ylim=(0.0, 1.0), xlabel="Recall", ylabel="Precision", title=title)
    ax.legend(loc="upper right", frameon=True, framealpha=0.92, fontsize=9.0)
    fig.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    plt.close(fig)


def _plot_calibration_by_site(predictions: pd.DataFrame, title: str) -> None:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 6.4))
    ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], linestyle="--", color="#9CA3AF", linewidth=1.2, label="Perfect calibration")
    for site in SITE_KEYS:
        site_df = predictions.loc[predictions["test_site"] == site]
        if site_df.empty:
            continue
        y_true = site_df["y_true"].to_numpy(dtype=int)
        y_prob = site_df["proba_death"].to_numpy(dtype=float)
        frac_pos, mean_pred = calibration_curve(y_true, y_prob, n_bins=8, strategy="quantile")
        brier = brier_score_loss(y_true, y_prob)
        ax.plot(
            mean_pred,
            frac_pos,
            color=SITE_COLORS[site],
            linewidth=2.3,
            marker="o",
            markersize=5,
            alpha=0.92,
            label=f"{SITE_LABEL.get(site, site)} (Brier {brier:.3f})",
        )
    ax.set_xlim(0.0, 1.0)
    ax.set_ylim(0.0, 1.0)
    ax.set_xlabel("Mean predicted probability")
    ax.set_ylabel("Observed frequency")
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.legend(loc="lower right", frameon=True, framealpha=0.92, fontsize=9.0)
    fig.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    plt.close(fig)


def _calibration_intercept_and_slope(y_true: np.ndarray, y_pred: np.ndarray) -> tuple[float, float]:
    y_true = np.asarray(y_true, dtype=int)
    y_pred = np.asarray(y_pred, dtype=float)
    probs = np.clip(y_pred, 1e-6, 1.0 - 1e-6)
    logits = np.log(probs / (1.0 - probs)).reshape(-1, 1)

    model = LogisticRegression(C=1e12, solver="lbfgs", fit_intercept=True, max_iter=2000)
    model.fit(logits, y_true)
    intercept = float(model.intercept_[0])
    slope = float(model.coef_.ravel()[0])
    return intercept, slope


def _model_predictions(model_key: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df = pd.read_csv(MODELS_DIR / "combined_all_predictions.csv")
    out = df.loc[df["model"] == model_key].copy()
    if out.empty:
        raise ValueError(f"No rows found for model={model_key!r} in combined_all_predictions.csv")
    return out


def _display_table(df: pd.DataFrame) -> None:
    display(df.style.hide(axis="index"))
```

## Methods

We evaluated the transportability of the REMAIN mortality model and, in response to evidence of calibration instability, assessed alternative models developed using a leave-one-site-out internal-external validation framework across the four participating sites. For the original REMAIN model, Site 2 was treated as the temporal validation cohort because it represented a later cohort from the development site, whereas Sites 1, 3, and 4 were treated as external validation cohorts.

### Models

For the REMAIN validation, the published logistic regression coefficients were applied unchanged to each patient in the validation dataset, without model refitting or recalibration. The REMAIN model included age and age squared together with categorical terms for sex, comorbidity burden, acute resuscitation plan (ARP), acuity-dependency profile, hospital admission in the previous 24 hours, and surgery in the previous 24 hours. 

For model re-development, we used a predictor set that was consistent with the prior insights from REMAIN about the utility of illness severity and care dependency in predicting in-hospital mortality. However, we simplified measurement of care dependency to a form that was more amenable to point of care measurement. In addition, the acuity-dependency profile was not included in the model. Instead, direct measurements of acuity and dependency were used. Predictors included: age, sex, ARP, hospital admission in the previous 24 hours, surgery in the previous 24 hours, ICU discharge in the previous 24 hours, MET call history in the previous 24 hours, NEWS, modified Index of Caring Dependency, and SOFA. A logistic regression model predicting in-hospital mortality with only the National Early Warning Score (NEWS) was used as a baseline comparator, because the NEWS is the tool currently used in practice to support escalation and decisions about seeking additional assistance for clinical deterioration. Candidate models were TabICL, group lasso logistic regression, group ridge logistic regression, AutoGluon tabular ensembles, and random forest. 

TabICL (In-context Learning) is a pre-trained transformer-based tabular foundation model and a form of prior-data fitted network. Unlike a conventional regression model, it does not estimate a new set of study-specific coefficients to be used for subsequent prediction. Instead, labelled patient records are supplied as contextual examples at the time predictions are generated, and the model estimates the probability of in-hospital mortality for a new patient by conditioning on the predictor-outcome relationships represented in those examples. In the present application, this was attractive because the relationships between mortality and the candidate predictors may be non-linear and may depend on higher-order interactions among physiologic severity measures and recent markers of instability, which would otherwise need to be specified manually within a parametric model. 

AutoGluon was used as an automated tabular prediction framework that trains multiple candidate learners and combines them through ensemble learning rather than selecting a single best model class. In each internal-external validation iteration, the pooled non-held-out sites were used to train candidate learners from gradient boosting machines, CatBoost, XGBoost, random forest, extremely randomized trees, k-nearest neighbours, and linear models. These models were combined using five-fold bagging and one level of stacked ensembling, so that out-of-fold predictions from the base learners could be used to train a higher-level weighted ensemble while limiting optimism from in-sample prediction. This approach was included to evaluate whether combining multiple tabular learning algorithms with different inductive biases would improve out-of-site performance and robustness relative to reliance on a single modelling framework. 

For the grouped penalised logistic models, continuous predictors were represented using spline terms and grouped penalties were applied so that related terms entered or shrank together. Grouping was used because several predictors were represented by multiple derived coefficients, including spline basis terms for continuous variables and sets of coefficients arising from categorical encoding. Penalising these related coefficients at the group level was intended to reduce unstable selection of isolated transformed terms, to preserve more coherent variable-level effects, and to retain a clinically interpretable linear modelling framework while still allowing greater flexibility than a conventional main-effects logistic regression.

An expanded set of predictors that included component-level NEWS, SOFA, and mICD variables was evaluated with TabICL and AutoGluon, as well as the random forest model. These approaches were considered better suited than the linear models to a larger and more granular predictor space because they can accommodate correlated component variables, non-linear effects, and higher-order interactions without requiring these structures to be specified a priori.

### Data analysis

Performance of the REMAIN model was assessed for the whole dataset as an overall measure of external validation as well as separately at each site. Site 2 was treated as a temporal validation subset of the total external validation dataset and Sites 1, 3, and 4 as strict external validation. Discrimination was summarised using the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC-AUC) and the area under the precision-recall curve (PR-AUC; average precision), overall prediction error using the Brier score, and calibration using calibration-in-the-large and calibration slope, estimated by regressing the observed outcome on the logit of the predicted probability. Site-level 95% confidence intervals for ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, Brier score, calibration-in-the-large, and calibration slope were estimated from 500 non-parametric bootstrap resamples. Pooled estimates were obtained across Sites 1, 3, and 4 using random-effects meta-analysis with Sidik-Jonkman heterogeneity estimation and Hartung-Knapp confidence and prediction intervals as a sensitivty analysis for external validation of the REMAIN model.  For ROC-AUC, within-site variance was estimated using the Hanley-McNeil method. PR-AUC and Brier score were pooled from site-specific estimates and corresponding standard errors.

The new models were developed using leave-one-site-out internal-external validation. In each iteration, one site was held out for testing and the candidate model was trained on the remaining three sites. This process was repeated so that each site served once as the external holdout. For group lasso and group ridge, the penalty parameter was re-selected within each non-held-out training set using inner 5-fold stratified cross-validation before evaluation in the held-out site. The spline basis for these grouped linear models was selected during preliminary model tuning by evaluating candidate specifications with 3, 4, or 5 knots and degree 2 or 3, ranked by validation ROC-AUC with Brier score used to break ties. The selected basis was then held fixed for the internal-external validation analyses (group lasso: 5 knots, degree 3; group ridge: 3 knots, degree 3). AutoGluon models were trained within the non-held-out sites and then refit on the full training data before generating holdout predictions. Model comparison focused on held-out-site ROC-AUC pooled across the four sites using the same random-effects meta-analytic framework, while PR-AUC, Brier score, calibration-in-the-large, and calibration slope were summarised descriptively at the site level. For overall discrimination and calibration figures, out-of-site predictions were concatenated across held-out sites for each model.

## REMAIN external and temporal validation summary

- **Outcome:** In-hospital mortality (DeathHospDisch)  
- **Model:** Logistic regression using the REMAIN coefficients
- **External validation sites:** Site 1, Site 3, Site 4  
- **Temporal validation site:** Site 2  
- **Primary metrics:** ROC-AUC, PR-AUC (average precision), Brier score, calibration slope/intercept.  

### Interpretation

- **Discrimination was moderate but varied across sites**, indicating that the predictor set retains useful ranking ability outside the development setting.  

- **Calibration slope suggested potential instability in transportability.**  
  - Temporal validation showed a slope <1 (0.825), indicating that predictions were too extreme, consistent with either overfitting or temporal changes in predictor–outcome relationships.  
  - External validation cohorts showed variable slope estimates, with one cohort demonstrating similar risk of overfitting to the development site (0.789) and another site at which estimated risks were too moderate.
  - Overall interpretation indicates that development model may have been overfitted to the development data, leading to overconfident predictions that did not generalize well. 

- **Uncertainty in calibration estimates:** Indicators of poor calibration were observed across temporal and external validation, suggesting that these findings may not be solely due to sampling variability.  

- **Brier score interpretation:** Brier scores were similar across cohorts and did not fully reflect differences in calibration, indicating that overall prediction error may remain comparable even when probability calibration differs.   

- **Rationale for a new model vs. only recalibration:** Variation in slope direction across cohorts (both <1 and >1) suggests that a single global recalibration is unlikely to fully address calibration differences.  

### Site-Level Validation Metrics

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| classes: plain

site_df = pd.read_csv(REMAIN_DIR / "table_2_site_level_metrics_with_ci.csv")
site_df = site_df.copy()
site_df["Validation type"] = site_df["validation_type"]
site_df["Site"] = site_df["site"].map(SITE_LABEL).fillna(site_df["site"])
site_df["val_rank"] = site_df["validation_type"].map({"External validation": 0, "Temporal validation": 1})
site_df["site_rank"] = site_df["site"].map(SITE_ORDER)
site_df = site_df.sort_values(["val_rank", "site_rank"]).reset_index(drop=True)

site_display = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "Validation type": site_df["Validation type"],
        "Site": site_df["Site"],
        "N": site_df["n"].astype(int),
        "Deaths": site_df["events"].astype(int),
        "CITL (intercept)": site_df["citl"].map(lambda v: fmt(v, 3)),
        "Calibration slope": site_df["slope"].map(lambda v: fmt(v, 3)),
        "ROC-AUC [95% CI]": site_df.apply(
            lambda r: fmt_ci(r["roc_auc"], r["roc_auc_ci_lower"], r["roc_auc_ci_upper"], 3), axis=1
        ),
        "PR-AUC [95% CI]": site_df.apply(
            lambda r: fmt_ci(r["pr_auc"], r["pr_auc_ci_lower"], r["pr_auc_ci_upper"], 3), axis=1
        ),
        "Brier [95% CI]": site_df.apply(
            lambda r: fmt_ci(r["brier"], r["brier_ci_lower"], r["brier_ci_upper"], 3), axis=1
        ),
    }
)
_display_table(site_display)
display(Markdown("**Site-level 95% confidence intervals were estimated from 500 nonparametric bootstrap replicates.**"))
```


### Random-Effects Meta-Analysis (Pooled External Validation)

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| classes: plain

meta_df = pd.read_csv(REMAIN_DIR / "table_1_internal_external_summary.csv")
meta_display = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "Metric": meta_df["Metric"],
        "Pooled estimate (95% CI)": meta_df.apply(
            lambda r: fmt_ci(r["Estimate"], r["CI lower"], r["CI upper"], 3), axis=1
        ),
        "95% PI": meta_df.apply(lambda r: fmt_range(r["PI lower"], r["PI upper"], 3), axis=1),
        "Tau²": meta_df["Tau²"].map(lambda v: fmt(v, 3)),
        "I² (%)": meta_df["I2 (%)"].map(lambda v: fmt(v, 1)),
    }
)
_display_table(meta_display)

remain_all = pd.read_csv(REMAIN_DIR / "combined_all_predictions.csv")
display(
    Markdown(
        f"**Overall sample:** N = {len(remain_all)}, deaths = {int(remain_all['y_true'].sum())}, "
        f"prevalence = {fmt(remain_all['y_true'].mean(), 3)}."
    )
)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Receiver operating characteristics curves for in-hospital mortality using the REMAIN model"

remain_pred = pd.read_csv(REMAIN_DIR / "combined_all_predictions.csv")
_plot_roc_by_site(remain_pred, "Receiver operating characteristics curves for in-hospital mortality using the REMAIN model")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Precision-recall curves for in-hospital mortality using the REMAIN model"

remain_pred = pd.read_csv(REMAIN_DIR / "combined_all_predictions.csv")
_plot_pr_by_site(remain_pred, "Precision-recall curves for in-hospital mortality using the REMAIN model")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Calibration plots for in-hospital mortality using the REMAIN model"

remain_pred = pd.read_csv(REMAIN_DIR / "combined_all_predictions.csv")
_plot_calibration_by_site(remain_pred, "Calibration plots for in-hospital mortality using the REMAIN model")
```

## Evaluating new models with internal-external validation

- The above results suggest that the REMAIN logistic regression model demonstrates reasonable discrimination but variable calibration across external and temporal validation cohorts.
- Investigated the performance of models and techniques that could reduce the risk of poor calibration when using a model at a new site. These included approaches to reduce risk of overfitting by using penalized regression techniques.
- Used  internal-external validation approach so that the model could be trained on data from multiple sites to reduce risk of overfitting to any single site.
- For group lasso and group ridge, regularization strength (\(\lambda\)) was selected within each non-holdout training set using inner 5-fold stratified cross-validation before scoring the held-out site.
- Also used a simplified measurement of care dependency and a set of predictors that are simpler to assess by a MET team at the time of decision-making and that we considered would be less susceptible to between-site measurement variability.


### Site-Level Holdout Results

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| classes: plain

site_holdout_base = pd.read_csv(MODELS_DIR / "table_s1_site_level_metrics.csv").copy()

merged_pred = pd.read_csv(MERGED_MODELS_DIR / "all_predictions.csv")
merged_model_label = {
    "tabicl": "TabICL (Expanded feature set)",
    "autogluon": "AutoGluon (Expanded feature set)",
    "random_forest": "Random forest (Expanded feature set)",
}

merged_rows: list[dict[str, float | str | int]] = []
for model_key, model_name in merged_model_label.items():
    for site in SITE_KEYS:
        site_df = merged_pred.loc[
            (merged_pred["model"] == model_key) & (merged_pred["test_site"] == site)
        ].copy()
        if site_df.empty:
            continue
        y_true = site_df["y_true"].to_numpy(dtype=int)
        y_prob = site_df["proba_death"].to_numpy(dtype=float)
        citl, slope = _calibration_intercept_and_slope(y_true, y_prob)
        merged_rows.append(
            {
                "Site": SITE_LABEL.get(site, site),
                "Model": model_name,
                "n": int(len(site_df)),
                "Events": int(site_df["y_true"].sum()),
                "Prevalence": float(site_df["y_true"].mean()),
                "ROC-AUC": float(roc_auc_score(y_true, y_prob)),
                "PR-AUC": float(average_precision_score(y_true, y_prob)),
                "Brier": float(brier_score_loss(y_true, y_prob)),
                "CITL": citl,
                "Slope": slope,
            }
        )

site_holdout_merged = pd.DataFrame(merged_rows)
news_pred = pd.read_csv(NEWS_LOGISTIC_DIR / "all_predictions.csv")
news_rows: list[dict[str, float | str | int]] = []
for site in SITE_KEYS:
    site_df = news_pred.loc[news_pred["test_site"] == site].copy()
    if site_df.empty:
        continue
    y_true = site_df["y_true"].to_numpy(dtype=int)
    y_prob = site_df["proba_death"].to_numpy(dtype=float)
    citl, slope = _calibration_intercept_and_slope(y_true, y_prob)
    news_rows.append(
        {
            "Site": SITE_LABEL.get(site, site),
            "Model": "NEWS logistic",
            "n": int(len(site_df)),
            "Events": int(site_df["y_true"].sum()),
            "Prevalence": float(site_df["y_true"].mean()),
            "ROC-AUC": float(roc_auc_score(y_true, y_prob)),
            "PR-AUC": float(average_precision_score(y_true, y_prob)),
            "Brier": float(brier_score_loss(y_true, y_prob)),
            "CITL": citl,
            "Slope": slope,
        }
    )

site_holdout_news = pd.DataFrame(news_rows)
site_holdout = pd.concat([site_holdout_base, site_holdout_merged, site_holdout_news], ignore_index=True)
site_holdout["site_rank"] = site_holdout["Site"].str.replace("Site ", "Site_", regex=False).map(SITE_ORDER)
site_holdout["model_rank"] = site_holdout["Model"].map(MODEL_ORDER)
site_holdout = site_holdout.sort_values(["site_rank", "ROC-AUC", "model_rank"], ascending=[True, False, True]).reset_index(drop=True)

for col in ["Prevalence", "ROC-AUC", "PR-AUC", "Brier", "CITL", "Slope"]:
    site_holdout[col] = site_holdout[col].map(lambda v: fmt(v, 4))

site_holdout["n"] = site_holdout["n"].astype(int)
site_holdout["Events"] = site_holdout["Events"].astype(int)
site_holdout_display = site_holdout[["Site", "n", "Events", "Prevalence", "Model", "ROC-AUC", "PR-AUC", "Brier", "CITL", "Slope"]].copy()
site_first_row = ~site_holdout_display["Site"].duplicated()
for col in ["Site", "n", "Events", "Prevalence"]:
    site_holdout_display[col] = site_holdout_display[col].where(site_first_row, "")
_display_table(site_holdout_display)
```

### Random-Effects Meta-Analysis (Pooled Across Sites)

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| classes: plain

model_meta_base = pd.read_csv(MODELS_DIR / "combined_summary_auc.csv")
model_label_base = {
    "tabicl": "TabICL",
    "group_lasso": "Group lasso",
    "group_ridge": "Group ridge",
    "autogluon": "AutoGluon",
    "random_forest": "Random forest",
}
model_meta_base["Model"] = model_meta_base["model"].map(model_label_base).fillna(model_meta_base["model"])

model_meta_merged = pd.read_csv(MERGED_MODELS_DIR / "summary_auc.csv")
model_label_merged = {
    "tabicl": "TabICL (Expanded feature set)",
    "autogluon": "AutoGluon (Expanded feature set)",
    "random_forest": "Random forest (Expanded feature set)",
}
model_meta_merged["Model"] = model_meta_merged["model"].map(model_label_merged).fillna(model_meta_merged["model"])

model_meta_news = pd.read_csv(NEWS_LOGISTIC_DIR / "summary_auc.csv")
model_meta_news["Model"] = model_meta_news["model"].map({"news_logistic": "NEWS logistic"}).fillna(
    model_meta_news["model"]
)

model_meta = pd.concat([model_meta_base, model_meta_merged, model_meta_news], ignore_index=True)
model_meta["model_rank"] = model_meta["Model"].map(MODEL_ORDER)
model_meta = model_meta.sort_values(["pooled_auc", "model_rank"], ascending=[False, True]).reset_index(drop=True)

model_meta_display = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "Model": model_meta["Model"],
        "Pooled ROC-AUC": model_meta["pooled_auc"].map(lambda v: fmt(v, 4)),
        "95% CI": model_meta.apply(lambda r: fmt_range(r["ci_95_lower"], r["ci_95_upper"], 4), axis=1),
        "95% PI": model_meta.apply(lambda r: fmt_range(r["pi_95_lower"], r["pi_95_upper"], 4), axis=1),
        "Tau² (logit AUC)": model_meta["tau2_logit_auc"].map(lambda v: fmt(v, 4)),
        "I² (%)": model_meta["i2_percent"].map(lambda v: fmt(v, 2)),
    }
)
_display_table(model_meta_display)
```

<small>Note: Prediction intervals in this internal-external validation context should not be directly compared to the pooled results for REMAIN external validation, as predictions for each hold-out site are from different models trained on data from each other site.</small>

### Interpretation

- TabICL resulted in the numerically highest pooled ROC-AUC. Additionally, the 95% prediction interval for TabICL were the most narrow across the models that were evaluated due to considerably less heterogeneity across sites. Inspection of measures to assess calibration also indicated that TabICL had the most consistent calibration across sites, with CITL values closest to 0 and slope values closest to 1 across sites.

- Developing a new model to predict in-hospital mortality after MET review using an internal-external validation approach, which allowed for training on data from multiple sites, did not substantially improve calibration compared to the REMAIN logistic regression model.

- On balance, given similar estimates of calibration, the TabICL model may be preferred given its slightly higher and more consistent performance in discrimination across sites.



### TabICL Holdout-Site Plots (Internal-External)

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Receiver operating characteristics curves for in-hospital mortality using the TabICL model"

tabicl_pred = _model_predictions("tabicl")
_plot_roc_by_site(tabicl_pred, "Receiver operating characteristics curves for in-hospital mortality using the TabICL model")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Precision-recall curves for in-hospital mortality using the TabICL model"

tabicl_pred = _model_predictions("tabicl")
_plot_pr_by_site(tabicl_pred, "Precision-recall curves for in-hospital mortality using the TabICL model")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Calibration plots for in-hospital mortality using the TabICL model"

tabicl_pred = _model_predictions("tabicl")
_plot_calibration_by_site(tabicl_pred, "Calibration plots for in-hospital mortality using the TabICL model")
```

### Group Ridge Holdout-Site Plots (Internal-External)

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Receiver operating characteristics curves for in-hospital mortality using the Group ridge model"

group_ridge_pred = _model_predictions("group_ridge")
_plot_roc_by_site(group_ridge_pred, "Receiver operating characteristics curves for in-hospital mortality using the Group ridge model")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Precision-recall curves for in-hospital mortality using the Group ridge model"

group_ridge_pred = _model_predictions("group_ridge")
_plot_pr_by_site(group_ridge_pred, "Precision-recall curves for in-hospital mortality using the Group ridge model")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Calibration plots for in-hospital mortality using the Group ridge model"
group_ridge_pred = _model_predictions("group_ridge")
_plot_calibration_by_site(group_ridge_pred, "Calibration plots for in-hospital mortality using the Group ridge model")
```
